title: Haitian Revolution Begins with the Bois Caïman Uprising
summary: On the stormy night of 21 to 22 August 1791, tens of thousands of enslaved workers on the northern plain of the French colony of Saint-Domingue rose against their masters, torched sugar and coffee plantations, and killed the whites they caught. A week earlier a Vodou ceremony at Bois Caïman, led by the houngan Dutty Boukman and the mambo Cécile Fatiman, had sworn the ringleaders to insurrection. What began as a spasm of destruction became the only successful large-scale slave revolution in modern history, produced the second independent republic in the Americas, and shook the Atlantic world.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
Saint-Domingue in 1789 was the richest slave colony in the world and one of the most brutal. Occupying the western third of the island of Hispaniola, it produced about forty per cent of the sugar and sixty per cent of the coffee consumed in Europe, along with indigo, cotton, and cacao, and its exports underwrote a great deal of eighteenth-century French prosperity: Bordeaux, Nantes, and La Rochelle grew wealthy on the trade. This wealth rested on a demographic pyramid that shocked even other slave societies. Some five hundred thousand enslaved Africans and their descendants worked the plantations, alongside roughly forty thousand whites, divided between the grands blancs of the plantocracy and the poorer petits blancs, and about twenty-eight thousand gens de couleur libres, free people mainly of mixed European and African descent, some of whom themselves owned plantations and slaves.

The conditions in which enslaved labourers worked were exceptionally lethal. Sugar cultivation in the tropics, with the punishing round of cane cutting and boiling, killed workers so fast that only continuous import of new captives from West and Central Africa kept the workforce numerically stable. Historians estimate that in the decade before the revolution around thirty thousand new captives were being landed each year at Cap-Français, the northern port then often called the Paris of the Antilles. Many of the enslaved were therefore African-born, still spoke their home languages, and preserved memories of political and military organisation in places like the Kongo kingdom, from whose civil wars a great many of them came. Maroon communities of escapees survived in the mountains, and the Vodou religion, a syncretism of West and Central African beliefs with elements of Catholicism, gave a language of resistance below the master's ear.

The outbreak of the French Revolution in 1789 lit the fuse. The Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen of 26 August 1789 proclaimed that all men were born free and equal in rights, without saying whether that included the colonies. Different constituencies in Saint-Domingue heard different things. The grands blancs, resentful of metropolitan trade monopolies, dreamed of colonial autonomy. Free people of colour, many of them wealthy and educated, demanded the same civic rights as whites; when the National Assembly in Paris passed a decree in May 1791 granting political rights to a limited group of mixed-race property owners, the white colonial assembly refused to publish it. Vincent Ogé, a wealthy free man of colour who had lobbied in Paris, returned to Saint-Domingue and led a small armed rising in the autumn of 1790 to enforce the decree; captured, he was broken on the wheel in Cap-Français in February 1791, an execution that horrified free and enslaved onlookers alike.

Among the enslaved, news of revolutionary language in France and of the political convulsions between whites and free people of colour circulated through carters, domestics, and market women. On the night of 14 August 1791, in a wooded clearing on Morne-Rouge above the plantation of Lenormand de Mézy, near the northern town of Le Cap, a gathering of coach drivers, foremen, and other trusted enslaved intermediaries met to plan a coordinated uprising. Sources from the early nineteenth century, principally the account of the mixed-race writer Antoine Dalmas published in 1814, describe a Vodou ceremony presided over by the Jamaican-born houngan Dutty Boukman and the mambo Cécile Fatiman, in which a black pig was sacrificed, its blood drunk, and an oath of secrecy and vengeance sworn during a thunderstorm. Modern scholars debate how much of the Dalmas account is embellishment, but the political meeting at Bois Caïman is generally accepted as historical, and it set the date of the general rising for a week later.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
On the night of 21 to 22 August 1791 the enslaved workers of the plaine du Nord, the fertile sugar plain that stretched inland from Cap-Français, put the plan into effect. Beginning on the Clément plantation near Acul, and spreading within hours across neighbouring estates, gangs of labourers armed with cane knives, machetes, agricultural tools, and a small number of firearms killed overseers and slave drivers, set fire to the cane fields and the great houses of their absent masters, and marched from plantation to plantation calling out the workforces. By dawn on 22 August the horizon north of the mountains was ringed with columns of smoke. The Chapuy engraving of 1795, made from a first-hand sketch by the artist Jean-Baptiste Boquet, shows a landscape black with burning plantations behind Cap-Français.

Within ten days the insurrection had swept across the entire northern plain, an area of roughly fifty by twenty-five kilometres containing some of the richest sugar estates in the world. Contemporary estimates suggest that between one thousand and two thousand whites were killed in the first weeks, hundreds of plantations were destroyed, and around eighty thousand enslaved workers had joined the rising. The insurgents fell back into camps in the mountainous interior, at places like Grande-Rivière and Dondon, where the terrain protected them and where they organised themselves into military units under leaders including Boukman himself, Jean-François Papillon, a former coachman, and Georges Biassou, both of whom would soon become the principal commanders in the north.

The white response was ferocious but disorganised. The garrison at Cap-Français, reinforced by militia, sortied against the insurgent camps and beheaded captured leaders, spiking heads on the walls of the town. Boukman was killed in combat in early November 1791 and his head was displayed publicly with a placard reading Chef des révoltés. But the insurgents held the countryside; whites and free people of colour fought among themselves in the west and south of the colony; and news of the northern rising took months to reach Paris, where the deputies of the constitutional monarchy were preoccupied with the flight of Louis XVI to Varennes and the shattering of the old order. When the National Assembly finally responded, it dispatched three commissioners to Saint-Domingue with authority to restore order, of whom the most consequential would be Léger-Félicité Sonthonax, a Jacobin lawyer.

The uprising also drew in the surrounding empires. Bourbon Spain, which controlled the eastern two-thirds of Hispaniola from Santo Domingo, quietly armed the insurgent bands as a way of undermining French power; Britain, at war with revolutionary France from 1793, would land troops in the western ports; and the French Republic itself, threatened by the loss of the colony and by internal warfare between whites, free people of colour, and enslaved, moved by stages towards abolition. On 29 August 1793, exactly two years after the uprising began, Sonthonax proclaimed general emancipation in the north of the colony; the National Convention in Paris ratified the abolition of slavery throughout the French empire on 4 February 1794. A former coachman turned insurgent officer, François-Dominique Toussaint Louverture, then in his early fifties and initially fighting for Spain, switched sides to the French Republic and began the military and political career that would carry him within seven years to the effective governorship of the entire colony.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Between 1791 and 1804 Saint-Domingue endured an almost unbroken war on many overlapping fronts: enslaved rebels against masters, free people of colour against whites, French Republicans against royalists, French forces against invading British and Spanish, and finally, from late 1801, a black army against a Napoleonic expeditionary force sent to restore slavery. Toussaint Louverture, appointed governor-general by the French Directory, promulgated a constitution in July 1801 that made him governor for life; Napoleon Bonaparte in response dispatched his brother-in-law General Charles Leclerc with an army of some twenty thousand French and allied troops. They landed in early 1802, seized the ports, and after months of fighting arrested Toussaint under a flag of truce in June 1802 and shipped him to France, where he died in the frigid Fort de Joux in the Jura in April 1803.

The French plan to reintroduce slavery, made public that summer, unified the black and mixed-race generals of the former French army in a final war of independence. Under the black general Jean-Jacques Dessalines and the mulatto general Alexandre Pétion, the insurgents inflicted a decisive defeat on the surviving French army under General Rochambeau at the Battle of Vertières outside Cap-Français on 18 November 1803. Yellow fever, which had already killed Leclerc, destroyed most of the rest of the French expeditionary force. On 1 January 1804 at Gonaïves, Dessalines proclaimed the independence of the country under its old Taíno name Haiti, the second independent republic in the Americas after the United States and the first anywhere to be founded by former slaves.

The human cost had been staggering. Standard estimates put Haitian dead in the twelve-year war at around two hundred thousand; French dead, largely from disease, at some seventy-five thousand; British dead at over forty thousand from an intervention that failed in 1798. Sugar production, which in 1789 had made Saint-Domingue France's richest possession, collapsed and would never recover to colonial levels. Under Dessalines and his successors Haiti reorganised as an independent republic (later briefly monarchies and empires), redistributed plantation land into smaller peasant holdings, and abolished slavery permanently in its constitution.

Internationally the revolution provoked a mixture of terror and inspiration. Slaveholders in the United States, Cuba, Brazil, and the British Caribbean tightened surveillance of their own enslaved populations, banned Haitian sailors from ports, and refused diplomatic recognition to the new republic for decades. In 1825 King Charles X of France recognised Haitian independence only in exchange for a crushing indemnity of 150 million francs, later reduced but not renegotiated in real terms, whose repayments burdened Haiti's public finances well into the twentieth century and whose modern equivalent has been estimated by economic historians at tens of billions of dollars. The revolution also directly precipitated the Louisiana Purchase: with his Caribbean base lost, Napoleon in 1803 sold France's vast North American territory to the United States for fifteen million dollars.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
The Haitian Revolution was the first anywhere in the Atlantic world to make good the universalist promise of the Rights of Man for the enslaved. Its 1805 constitution under Dessalines banned slavery in perpetuity, made all Haitian citizens legally black regardless of colour, and forbade whites from owning land in the country. That principle, and the fact that former slaves had defeated the armies of three European empires, was known everywhere in the Americas that slavery still existed. Abolitionists in Britain and the United States cited Haiti in the arguments that led to the ending of the British slave trade in 1807, the emancipation of British colonial slaves in 1833, and eventually the American Civil War in the 1860s. Simón Bolívar, sheltered in Haiti in 1815 and 1816, received military aid from President Alexandre Pétion on the condition that he emancipate the slaves in the territories he liberated; when Gran Colombian independence was consolidated, Bolívar kept his promise.

For a long time European and North American historiography kept Haiti at arm's length. The Trinidadian historian C. L. R. James in his 1938 book The Black Jacobins told the story to a mid-twentieth-century Anglophone readership for the first time in the language of world revolution; his was the reading that inspired later generations of African independence leaders. The Haitian anthropologist Michel-Rolph Trouillot in his 1995 Silencing the Past argued that western historiography had systematically suppressed the revolution precisely because it violated the imagined boundary of who could be an agent of Enlightenment universalism.

Inside Haiti the memory of 1791 has been carried at the annual commemoration of the Bois Caïman ceremony every August, an event of both national and Vodou religious significance. The country pays a heavy contemporary price for the revolution as well as claiming its glory: the indemnity, the diplomatic isolation, the destruction of infrastructure, and later foreign occupations have contributed to conditions of poverty that have made Haiti, in the twenty-first century, one of the poorest states in the western hemisphere. That the country is nonetheless still the standing proof that the enslaved could free themselves is the difficult double legacy of the uprising that began in the burning cane fields of the northern plain in August 1791.

worldContext.text: China in 1791 was in the last decade of the long reign of the Qianlong Emperor of the Qing dynasty, who had come to the throne in 1735 and would formally abdicate in 1796. The empire was outwardly at the apex of its power, having absorbed Xinjiang in the 1750s and completed a large-scale invasion of Nepal in 1791 that reached Kathmandu and forced the Gurkha regime to submit to tribute. Below the surface, however, official corruption under the notorious favourite Heshen and rural distress were accumulating; the White Lotus rebellion would break out in 1794. Japan, meanwhile, was governed by the young Tokugawa shogun Ienari from Edo, with Dutch traders confined to the artificial island of Dejima at Nagasaki as the only European contact.

The Indian subcontinent in 1791 was in the middle of the Third Anglo-Mysore War between the Sultanate of Mysore under Tipu Sultan and a British East India Company alliance including the Marathas and the Nizam of Hyderabad. Lord Cornwallis, who had surrendered at Yorktown in 1781, was now governor-general of Bengal and personally led the campaign that in 1792 would besiege Tipu in his capital Seringapatam and dictate the humiliating Treaty of Seringapatam. Northern India remained a patchwork of Maratha chiefs, Sikh states in the Punjab, and a diminished Mughal court at Delhi. In Nepal the young Rana Bahadur Shah had provoked the Qing intervention noted above.

The Ottoman Empire in 1791 was ending a disastrous war with Habsburg Austria and Russia. The Treaty of Sistova with Austria signed on 4 August 1791 restored the pre-war frontiers, while the war with Catherine the Great's Russia would end in January 1792 at Jassy with the cession of the Black Sea coast including the fortress of Ochakov. Sultan Selim III, on the throne since 1789, was already drafting the Nizam-i Cedid reforms intended to modernise the janissary corps and provincial administration. Napoleon's invasion of Egypt was still seven years off; Cairo was ruled in practice by feuding Mamluk beys nominally subject to Istanbul.

West Africa in 1791 was still the source of most captives shipped to the Americas. The kingdom of Dahomey under King Agonglo continued to raid its neighbours and sell prisoners to European traders at the ports of Ouidah and Porto Novo; Asante, further inland, dominated the gold and kola trade of what is now Ghana. The Kongo kingdom in west-central Africa, from which many Saint-Domingue insurgents ultimately came, remained fragmented by decades of civil war among its rival ruling houses, feeding the Atlantic trade. In the interior of southern Africa the Zulu clans that would soon consolidate under Shaka were still small chiefdoms among many. In the Cape Colony Dutch settlers were expanding into Xhosa territory.

In 1791 the British penal colony at Sydney Cove, founded in January 1788 with the First Fleet, was just three and a half years old and still living precariously off imported stores. Governor Arthur Phillip presided over a settlement of some three thousand convicts, marines, and settlers; the Aboriginal Eora people of the harbour had been devastated by a smallpox epidemic in 1789. Small British vessels were also exploring the coasts of Tasmania and New Zealand. Across the wider Pacific, indigenous kingdoms in Hawaii under Kamehameha I were on the verge of unification, and Tongan, Samoan, and Tahitian societies remained politically independent even as European contact accelerated. The Mutiny on the Bounty had happened only two years earlier.

figures.name: Dutty Boukman
figures.role: Jamaican-born Vodou houngan and coachman on the Clément plantation, principal leader of the 22 August 1791 uprising, killed in combat in November 1791

figures.name: Cécile Fatiman
figures.role: Vodou mambo of mixed Corsican and African parentage who presided with Boukman over the Bois Caïman ceremony on 14 August 1791

figures.name: Toussaint Louverture
figures.role: Former coachman on the Bréda plantation who joined the insurrection in late 1791, rose to command all republican forces in Saint-Domingue, and served as governor-general until his arrest in 1802
figures.credit: 19th-century engraving, New York Public Library / Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Jean-Jacques Dessalines
figures.role: General under Louverture and later commander of the war of independence; declared Haitian independence on 1 January 1804 and became the country's first head of state as Emperor Jacques I

figures.name: Léger-Félicité Sonthonax
figures.role: French Republican civil commissioner who proclaimed the abolition of slavery in northern Saint-Domingue on 29 August 1793

images.caption: Toussaint Louverture, a mid-19th-century engraving of the former Bréda coachman who rose to command the Republican armies of Saint-Domingue and to govern the colony until his arrest by French forces in June 1802.
images.credit: Unknown 19th-century engraver, Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture, New York Public Library, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: The Battle of Vertières, 18 November 1803, at which the army of Jean-Jacques Dessalines defeated the last French expeditionary force outside Cap-Français, opening the way to Haitian independence six weeks later.
images.credit: Modern reproduction of period imagery, via Wikimedia Commons (uploader Andre10psm)
